sentences.base_language: I woke up this morning and didn't feel well.
sentences.target_language: 오늘 아침에 일어났는데 몸이 안 좋았어요.
sentences.romanized: Oneul achime ireonannneunde momi an joasseoyo.
sentences.literal: Today morning at woke-up-but body not good-was.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-았/었는데** (-at/eotneunde) connects two clauses with a background + result or contrast relationship: 일어났는데 = 'I woke up, and (then)...'
Unlike -고 (neutral sequence), **-는데/-았는데** sets up a background situation that leads to or contrasts with what follows.
**몸이 안 좋았어요** = 'my body was not good' — the standard Korean way to say 'I didn't feel well.' 몸 (body) + 이 (subject marker) + 안 좋았어요 (was not good).

sentences.base_language: My throat was sore and my nose was stuffy.
sentences.grammarNote: -고 (go) connects two adjective or verb clauses in a neutral list: 아프고 (sore and...), 막혔고 (blocked and...). It does not imply cause or contrast — just lists facts. Compare: 목이 아파서 (because my throat hurts...) which adds a causal link.

sentences.base_language: I also had a slight fever.
sentences.grammarNote:
**도** (do) = 'also/too', placed right after the noun: 열도 = 'fever too / also a fever.'
**열이 있다** (yeori itda) = 'to have a fever' — literally 'fever exists.' Korean uses 있다/없다 to express having or not having physical symptoms: 기침이 있어요 (I have a cough), 열이 없어요 (no fever).
**조금** (jogeum) = 'a little / slightly' — a very common degree adverb used to soften statements.

sentences.base_language: Because I felt sick, I decided to stay home.
sentences.literal: Body sick-because home in stay decided.
sentences.grammarNote: -아/어서 (a/eoseo) expresses cause or sequence: 아파서 = 'because (I am) sick.' The verb stem 아프다 ends in 아, so it takes 아서. This connector cannot be used with past tense — the tense is expressed only in the final verb. 아파서 집에 있었어요 ✓, but NOT 아팠어서 ✗.

sentences.base_language: I took some cold medicine with a glass of water.
sentences.target_language: 물 한 잔과 함께 감기약을 먹었어요.
sentences.romanized: Mul han jangwa hamkke gamgiyageul meogeosseoyo.
sentences.literal: Water one glass-with together cold-medicine ate.
sentences.grammarNote:
**감기약** (gamgiyak) = cold medicine — 감기 (cold) + 약 (medicine). **약을 먹다** (yageul meokda) is the standard phrase 'to take medicine' — literally 'to eat medicine.'
**과 함께** (gwa hamkke) = 'together with / along with' — the formal version of 랑 같이. After consonants: 과 함께; after vowels: 와 함께.
**잔** (jan) = counter for cups/glasses. 물 한 잔 = one glass of water; 커피 두 잔 = two cups of coffee.

sentences.base_language: Then I made some warm honey tea.
sentences.grammarNote:
**그다음에** (geudaeume) = 'after that / then' — used to sequence steps in a narrative or routine.
**따뜻하다** (ttatteutada) = 'to be warm (temperature)' — the modifier form is 따뜻한: 따뜻한 차 (warm tea).
Contrast: 따뜻하다 (warm — pleasant, physical) vs. 포근하다 (cozy/warm — emotional feeling).
**꿀차** (kkul-cha) = honey tea — 꿀 (honey) + 차 (tea). Honey tea is a popular Korean home remedy for sore throats and colds.

sentences.base_language: If I drink warm tea, I always feel a little better.
sentences.target_language: 따뜻한 차를 마시면 항상 조금 나아져요.
sentences.romanized: Ttatteuthan chareul masimyeon hangsang jogeum naajyeoyo.
sentences.literal: Warm tea drink-if always a-little get-better.
sentences.grammarNote: -(으)면 ((eu)myeon) is the conditional: 마시면 = 'if (I) drink.' Stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ use -면 directly; stems ending in another consonant add -으면. Examples: 가다 → 가면 (if go), 먹다 → 먹으면 (if eat), 자다 → 자면 (if sleep).

sentences.base_language: I lay down in bed and rested for a while.
sentences.grammarNote:
**눕다** (nupda) = 'to lie down' — an irregular ㅂ verb: 누워요 (present), 누웠어요 (past). The ㅂ changes to 우 before a vowel: 눕 → 누워서.
**누워서** uses -아서/어서 to chain the action: 'lay down and (then) rested.'
**잠시** (jamsi) = 'for a moment / briefly' — a common adverb. Compare: 조금 (a little), 잠깐 (for a sec/moment), 잠시 (briefly/for a short while — slightly more formal than 잠깐).

sentences.base_language: My friend heard I was sick and came to visit.
sentences.target_language: 친구가 제가 아프다는 걸 듣고 방문했어요.
sentences.romanized: Chinguga jega apeudaneun geol deutgo bangmunhaesseoyo.
sentences.literal: Friend I sick-that thing heard-and visited.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-다는 것** (-daneun geot) is the indirect speech nominalisation: 아프다는 걸 = 'the thing that (I) am sick.' Used to report or refer to a statement.
**듣다** (deutda) = 'to hear' — an irregular ㄷ verb: 들어요 (present), 들었어요 (past). The ㄷ changes to ㄹ before a vowel.
**방문하다** (bangmunhada) = 'to visit / come to see someone' — a more formal term; the casual version is 놀러 오다 (come to hang out).

sentences.base_language: She brought a warm pot of chicken soup.
sentences.grammarNote:
**가져오다** (gajyeooda) = 'to bring' — 가져 (carry) + 오다 (come). The past tense is 가져왔어요.
Contrast: **가져오다** (bring here, toward speaker) vs. **가져가다** (take away, away from speaker).
**닭고기** (dalkgogi) = chicken meat — 닭 (chicken) + 고기 (meat). Korea's popular **삼계탕** (samgyetang, ginseng chicken soup) is a famous cold and heat remedy.
**국** (guk) = soup/broth — a lighter soup, distinct from 찌개 (jjigae, thick stew).

sentences.base_language: I was really touched. Good friends are the best medicine.
sentences.target_language: 정말 감동받았어요. 좋은 친구가 최고의 약이에요.
sentences.romanized: Jeongmal gamongbadasseoyo. Joeun chinguga choego-ui yagieyo.
sentences.literal: Really moved-was. Good friend best medicine-is.
sentences.grammarNote: 아프다 (apeuda) = to be sick / to hurt in a general sense. 쑤시다 (ssusida) = to ache deeply, throb, or feel a dull-sharp pain — used for muscles, joints, or bones. 목이 아파요 (my throat hurts) uses 아프다, while 근육이 쑤셔요 (my muscles ache) uses 쑤시다. Knowing the distinction sounds more natural and precise.

sentences.base_language: By evening I felt a little better thanks to the rest and soup.
sentences.target_language: 저녁이 되자 쉬고 국을 먹은 덕분에 조금 나아졌어요.
sentences.romanized: Jeonyeogi doeja swigo gugeul meogeun deokbune jogeum naajyeosseoyo.
sentences.literal: Evening became rest-and soup ate thanks-to a-little better-became.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-자** (ja) indicates that something happens immediately after the preceding action: 저녁이 되자 = 'as soon as it became evening.'
**덕분에** (deokbune) = 'thanks to / owing to' — used for positive causes: 쉬고 국을 먹은 덕분에 = 'thanks to resting and eating soup.'
**나아지다** (naajida) = 'to get better / improve' — 나아지다 + 었어요 → 나아졌어요 (became better). The -지다 ending turns adjectives into change-of-state verbs: 좋아지다 (become good), 나빠지다 (get worse).
